claimText: The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 covers all biological resources within India, making its scope broader than that of the Nagoya Protocol.
evidenceExcerpt: BD Act 2002 covers all biological resources in India, which is broader than the Nagoya Protocol.
examUse: Statement-based question comparing the scope of the BD Act and the Nagoya Protocol.
distractorHooks: The Nagoya Protocol has a broader scope than the BD Act
The BD Act only covers threatened or endemic biological resources

claimText: The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 is implemented through a three-tier statutory system consisting of the National Biodiversity Authority (NBA), State Biodiversity Boards (SBBs), and local Biodiversity Management Committees (BMCs).
evidenceExcerpt: Implemented via a three-tier system: NBA (Central), SBBs (State), and BMCs (Local).
examUse: Statement-based question on the institutional architecture of biodiversity governance in India.
distractorHooks: The BD Act operates on a two-tier system without local committees
BMCs operate at the state level

claimText: The National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) possesses the powers of a civil court.
evidenceExcerpt: National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) possesses the powers of a civil court.
examUse: Statement-based question regarding the statutory powers of environmental bodies in India.
distractorHooks: The NBA is purely an advisory body with no judicial powers
The NBA possesses the powers of a criminal court

claimText: The Kani tribal community of Kerala participates in benefit sharing for the Jeevani drug, which is developed from the Arogyapacha plant.
evidenceExcerpt: Kani tribal community of Kerala shares benefits from the Jeevani drug developed from the Arogyapacha plant.
examUse: Correct-pair question matching traditional communities to their associated biological resources or ABS case studies.
distractorHooks: The Kani tribe is native to Central India
The Jeevani drug is derived from the Neem tree
